name: sofritas
instructions:
BROIL THE POBLANO: char the whole poblano pepper over a gas flame or under the broiler until the skin is blistered and blackened on all sides (about 4 minutes over flame, 5+5 under broiler). No need to peel or remove the seeds. Set aside.

COOK THE AROMATICS: heat 1 1/2 tbsp olive oil in a frying pan over medium-high heat. Add finely chopped white onion and a pinch of kosher salt. Cook 5-6 min until just starting to color. Add chopped garlic, cook 2 min. Add tomato paste, optional chopped fresh tomatoes (as much or as little as you want — see notes on the flexible tomato rule), cumin, oregano, ancho + guajillo chili powder (see notes), dried porcini mushroom powder (~1/2 tsp — see notes on porcini as glutamate carrier), kosher salt, and black pepper. Cook 1 min.

DEGLAZE: splash in red or white wine and let it reduce briefly with the aromatics. Then add the vinegar (red or white wine vinegar, both work). Clean out the pan after.

BLEND THE SAUCE: transfer the aromatic-spice mixture to a blender. Add the broiled poblano, chipotle chiles (in adobo), and 1/2 cup water. Blend until relatively smooth. Taste and adjust.

CRUMBLE AND COOK THE TOFU: cut super firm tofu into slabs and squeeze out any water. Crumble into blueberry-sized pieces. Heat 1 1/2 tbsp olive oil in the cleaned pan over medium-high heat. Add the tofu crumbles in a single packed layer, season with kosher salt. Cook undisturbed 2-3 min, then continue cooking ~12 min total, stirring only every 2-3 min, until most of the tofu is DEEPLY BROWNED — push this further than you might think (see crispy-fry note below).

SIMMER WITH SAUCE: pour the blended sauce over the tofu. Simmer 5 min, stirring occasionally, then reduce heat and cook another 5 min until thickened and saucy. Add splashes of water if it gets too thick.

FINISH: taste and adjust. Squeeze of lime juice at the end.
notes:
From Nisha Vora (Rainbow Plant Life) — her Chipotle sofritas copycat. A few adjustments from the original below.

TOMATO — ANYTHING GOES, TOMATO PASTE + AS MUCH OR AS LITTLE REAL TOMATO AS YOU WANT: this is one of the fun things about sofritas — the tomato slot is genuinely flexible. Tomato paste alone works. Tomato paste + 2 chopped fresh tomatoes (Nisha's original) works. Tomato paste + 4 tomatoes also works — you just get a fruitier, more tomato-forward sauce. Skip the fresh entirely and lean on paste for a more concentrated, chipotle-forward result. All of these are valid moves; the recipe holds its shape across the range. Treat the fresh tomato quantity as a knob you can turn based on mood and what's in the pantry.

ADD REAL WINE: a splash of red or white wine deglazing the aromatic-spice mixture adds depth that pure-vinegar can't. Either color works. This is a leftover-friendly slot — pour in whatever open bottle is in the fridge.

VINEGAR + WINE — CROSS-PAIRING RULE (PAIR OPPOSITE COLORS): red wine vinegar and white wine vinegar both work here, and similarly red wine and white wine both work for the deglaze. The specific pairing rule: pair red wine with white wine vinegar, OR white wine with red wine vinegar — either cross-pairing works. Same-color pairings (red+red or white+white) read one-note; the cross-pairing gives you a fuller acid-and-fermentation profile from each half of the wine shelf. Whatever you have open in the fridge is fine; just pair opposite colors between the wine and vinegar slots.

SUPER FIRM TOFU: the cookbook's tofu article calls out crumbles for tacos as the one legitimate use of extra/super firm tofu (otherwise home-pressed firm is preferred). Sofritas is that case — you want dry, dense crumbles that hold their shape against the sauce. Don't substitute regular firm tofu without pressing aggressively first and adding cornstarch.

GLUTAMATES / NO STOCK REQUIRED — ADOBO + PORCINI + TOMATO PASTE CARRY IT: sofritas doesn't need stock. The umami/glutamate slot is fully filled by three ingredients working together:
- CHIPOTLE IN ADOBO: the smoky-tangy fermented adobo sauce is a serious glutamate carrier alongside the smokiness. This is often overlooked in the chipotle discussion (which focuses on heat) — the adobo is doing structural umami work.
- DRIED PORCINI MUSHROOM POWDER: adds a huge glutamate lift and a background woodsy depth that reads as 'meaty' in a non-meat dish. About 1/2 tsp is enough; don't overdo it or it starts reading italian/mushroom-forward. Non-optional.
- TOMATO PASTE: cooked briefly with the aromatics for concentrated tomato umami.
Stock is not needed and would actually thin the flavor concentration — the sauce is meant to be dense and coating, not brothy. Water is the only liquid to add if the sauce reduces too far.

OLIVE OIL IS A MAJOR FLAVOR CONTRIBUTOR — USE GOOD EVOO: sofritas leans on olive oil more than most mexican dishes do. The 1 1/2 tbsp for the aromatics and the 1 1/2 tbsp for the tofu are both doing real flavor work — a peppery, grassy real EVOO gives the sauce depth that neutral oil doesn't. Don't use vegetable oil here; the olive oil is one of the character-carriers.

CRISPY-FRY THE TOFU — PUSH THE BROWNING PAST WHAT FEELS RIGHT: I like the tofu crumbles taken further than Nisha's recipe suggests. Not just browned in patches, but genuinely CRISPY on the exterior of most pieces — the maillard character and the textural crunch against the saucy exterior of the finished dish is a real upgrade. This means longer in the pan before the sauce goes in (or optionally: fry the tofu crumbles separately and hotter, more like a proper crispy tofu, then fold into the sauce at the end).

CHIPOTLE HEAT: 1 chipotle chile + 1/2 tbsp adobo sauce is the default. Cut the adobo sauce to 1/2 tsp for a milder version.

DON'T SKIP THE POBLANO BROIL: broiling adds the smoky undertone the dish depends on. Raw poblano blended in is noticeably flatter.

WHITE ONIONS OVER YELLOW: Nisha's recipe says yellow or red onion. White onion is the better choice — sharper and cleaner against the smoky-tomato base, more characteristically mexican. Yellow works but reads softer than the dish wants.

CHILI POWDER — MIX OF ANCHO + GUAJILLO IS BEST (PRIMARY); NEW MEXICO OK BUT SECONDARY: after more testing, the best chile powder is a mix of ancho and guajillo — the two together give the sauce a richer character than either alone. Rough character breakdown:
- ANCHO CHILI POWDER: darker, raisin-sweet, richer. The base note; carries the depth. Nisha's original recipe called for ancho alone.
- GUAJILLO CHILI POWDER: brighter, fruit-forward, slightly tangy. Adds lift and complexity to the ancho base. Together they read like a proper mexican chile-mix rather than a single-note powder.
- NEW MEXICO CHILI POWDER: still fine as a substitute or supplement — cleaner heat, fresher pepper character — but demoted to secondary. Use it if you don't have guajillo, or add a small amount alongside ancho+guajillo for extra warmth. Not the first reach.
Generic chili powder is also acceptable as a fallback but the ancho+guajillo combination is a clear step up.
